questions.question: Define thrashing and explain why it degrades system performance.
keyPoints: Thrashing is excessive page fault activity causing constant paging
System spends more time on I/O than actual computation
CPU utilization drops drastically as it waits for page operations
Response time increases making system unusable

questions.question: Explain the working set model and its relationship to thrashing.
keyPoints: Working set is set of pages actively used by a process
Defined by window size - pages referenced in recent time
If working set size > allocated frames, process thrashes
Goal is to keep working set in memory to prevent thrashing

questions.question: Describe the page fault frequency (PFF) control technique for thrashing prevention.
keyPoints: Monitor page fault rate for each process
If fault rate too high → allocate more frames
If fault rate too low → reclaim excess frames
Maintains system at optimal performance point

questions.question: What is process suspension as a thrashing control technique?
keyPoints: Complete process swapped out to disk
All frames occupied by suspended process are freed
Other processes can use freed frames
Suspended process resumes when memory pressure reduces

questions.question: Explain the relationship between degree of multiprogramming and thrashing.
keyPoints: More processes in memory = higher CPU utilization up to a point
Beyond optimal point, each process gets fewer frames
Insufficient frames cause more page faults
Eventually leads to thrashing and lower CPU utilization

questions.question: Differentiate between local and global page replacement in the context of thrashing.
keyPoints: Local: process can only replace its own allocated pages
Global: any process can replace any page in free pool
Local prevents interference but may not be optimal
Global more flexible but one thrashing process can affect all

questions.question: What is prepaging and how does it help in managing thrashing?
keyPoints: Prepaging loads pages into memory before they are actually needed
Based on prediction of which pages will be needed
When suspended process resumes, pages already in memory
Reduces initial page fault burst and prevents immediate thrashing

questions.question: Explain the causes of thrashing in virtual memory systems. Discuss various techniques to control and prevent thrashing.
keyPoints: Causes: insufficient frames, excessive page faults, working set > allocated frames
Working Set Model: keep active pages in memory
Page Fault Frequency: monitor and control fault rates
Process Suspension: swap out entire processes
Prepaging: load pages in advance
Global vs Local Replacement strategies
Maintaining optimal degree of multiprogramming

questions.question: Draw and explain the CPU utilization vs degree of multiprogramming curve. How does this graph help in understanding thrashing?
keyPoints: X-axis: Degree of Multiprogramming, Y-axis: CPU Utilization
Curve rises initially as more processes utilize CPU better
Peak point represents optimal multiprogramming
Beyond peak, curve drops sharply due to thrashing
Thrashing zone: too many processes, too few frames each
System administrator should operate near peak point
Adding more processes past this point degrades performance

questions.question: Discuss the working set model of memory management. How does it help in preventing thrashing? What are its limitations?
keyPoints: Working set = pages referenced in recent time window (τ)
WS(t) = set of pages referenced in interval [t-τ, t]
Process thrashes if WS size > allocated frames
Keep WS in memory to avoid thrashing
Advantages: provides theoretical foundation, adaptive allocation
Limitations: tracking working set is complex, window size hard to choose
May not adapt quickly to changing program behavior

questions.question: Explain the concept of page fault frequency control in detail. How does it work and what are its advantages over other thrashing control techniques?
keyPoints: Monitor page fault rate per process
Define upper and lower thresholds for fault rate
If faults > upper threshold: allocate more frames to process
If faults < lower threshold: remove excess frames from process
Simple to implement and directly addresses cause
More dynamic than working set model
Allows fine-grained control per process
Can automatically adjust to changing workloads
